scenario: Aurora Bank tracks job polling rates on a Camunda 8.8 Java worker that activates 6500 jobs per day. The platform engineering team wants the ActivateJobsCommand polling count exported to Prometheus today without waiting for a built in counter, and prefers an approach that does not change the broker configuration.
question: Which Camunda 8.8 documented workaround lets Aurora Bank track the ActivateJobsCommand polling count from a Java job worker?

options.text: Register a gRPC ClientInterceptor or an Apache HttpClient AsyncExecChainHandler on the Camunda client builder so the interceptor counts every ActivateJobsCommand invocation issued by the Aurora Bank Java worker on its client channel

Enable a camunda.client.metrics.activateJobs property on the JobWorkerBuilder so the Java client publishes a built in polling counter directly to the configured Micrometer MeterRegistry at Aurora Bank without any interceptors

Set zeebe.broker.metrics.activateJobs to true in the broker configuration so Zeebe exports the polling rate of every connected Java worker through the orchestration cluster monitoring endpoint used by Aurora Bank

Subclass the BackoffSupplier and increment a Micrometer Counter inside its method so the Aurora Bank Java worker treats every retry delay calculation as a single ActivateJobsCommand polling event for the metric

a.text: Correct. The documented workaround uses a gRPC ClientInterceptor or Apache HttpClient AsyncExecChainHandler attached to the Camunda client builder to track client calls, including the ActivateJobsCommand invocation issued each time the worker polls for jobs.

b.text: Incorrect. The Java client does not expose a built in activate jobs polling counter on the JobWorkerBuilder; the documented option is the gRPC ClientInterceptor or HttpClient chain handler workaround.

c.text: Incorrect. The broker does not expose a per client polling rate metric via a zeebe.broker.metrics.activateJobs flag; tracking is done on the client side through an interceptor or chain handler.

d.text: Incorrect. The BackoffSupplier only computes retry delays after failed polls and does not run on every poll, so it cannot be used as a proxy for the ActivateJobsCommand polling count.

explanation: Camunda 8.8 deliberately limits the built in JobWorkerMetrics to jobs activated and jobs handled. To track additional metrics like the ActivateJobsCommand polling count, the docs recommend attaching a gRPC ClientInterceptor or an Apache HttpClient AsyncExecChainHandler to the client builder.
